description: This figure presents a regression analysis chart illustrating the influence of chemical composition on the elastic modulus of martensitic and ferritic steels, with phase composition and microstructure neglected. The horizontal axis lists alloying elements sorted by decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i): Fe, Cr, Mo, Si, Ni, Mn, Cu, and C. The vertical axis displays the regression coefficient r_i in GPa, quantifying each element's contribution per mass percent to the elastic modulus. Solid black squares with error bars represent the regression coefficients derived from this study (error bars indicate standard errors), while open circles denote corresponding literature data points. The chart reveals that Si exhibits the highest positive regression coefficient (~4.6 GPa), followed by Cr (~2.7 GPa), Mo (~2.6 GPa), and Cu (~2.4 GPa). In contrast, C shows a negative coefficient (~-0.5 GPa), indicating a reduction in elastic modulus, while Ni demonstrates a negligible to slightly negative effect. Mn and Fe display modest positive contributions. As stated in the caption, this analysis encompasses 53 batches of steel; Cr, Mo, and Si significantly increase the elastic modulus, whereas C and Ni decrease it, with Mn and Cu being statistically insignificant in the investigated concentration ranges. The regression model exhibits a standard error of 1.2 GPa and an R² value of 0.96.

This figure presents a regression analysis chart (Figure 2) showing the influence of chemical composition on the elastic modulus of ferritic steels, with elemental concentrations in ferrite and cementite content calculated using Thermo-Calc. The horizontal axis lists elements sorted by decreasing statistical significance ($r_i/\sigma_i$), ordered from Fe and cementite (left) through Cr, Mo, Si, and Mn to Cu (right). The vertical axis displays the regression coefficient $r_i$ in GPa, quantifying each element's contribution to the elastic modulus. Black horizontal bars with vertical error bars represent the regression coefficients and their standard errors, while open circles indicate individual literature data points. The plot demonstrates that Si (~5.5 GPa) and Mn (~5.4 GPa) exhibit the highest positive regression coefficients, significantly increasing the elastic modulus, followed by Mo (~3.9 GPa), Cr (~2.2 GPa), and Cu (~2.7 GPa). Crucially, cementite shows a regression coefficient (~2.1 GPa) nearly identical to that of pure Fe (~2.1 GPa), indicating that cementite content has virtually no effect on the elastic modulus, as confirmed by the caption. The large error bars for Cu render its weak positive effect statistically insignificant. These results support predictive modeling of steel elastic modulus based on composition, with cementite acting as a neutral phase.

This figure presents a bar-and-error plot illustrating the regression coefficients (r_i) quantifying the influence of various alloying elements and carbide phases on the elastic modulus of austenitic steels, with units in GPa. The x-axis lists components sorted by decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i), including Fe, Cr, Ni, NbC, M_{23}C_6, TiC, N, and Mo. The y-axis displays the magnitude of the regression coefficients. Nitrogen exhibits the highest coefficient (~15 GPa) but features exceptionally large error bars indicating low statistical significance due to limited available data (only four steels contained considerable N). TiC (~7.3 GPa) and NbC (~6.5 GPa) demonstrate strong positive effects on the elastic modulus with moderate uncertainty. Chromium shows a positive coefficient (~2.5 GPa), while Ni and Mo display lower values (~2 GPa and ~0.5 GPa respectively), consistent with their reported modulus-reducing behavior. Notably, M_{23}C_6 exhibits a coefficient similar to the Fe baseline (~2 GPa), confirming its negligible practical effect despite statistical significance. The visualization supports the regression model (R²=0.83, standard error 2.9 GPa) derived from 24 batches of austenitic steels with phase contents calculated using Thermo-Calc, demonstrating that elastic modulus can be predicted from chemical composition and phase constitution.
